title: Nalanda gears up to tackle AES, JE and heatwave - The Times of India
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Delhi
full_text:
Biharsharif: Nalanda district magistrate (DM) Kundan Kumar on Wednesday chaired a review meeting with officials of the district health department to assess preparedness for prevention and management of Acute Encephalitis Syndrome (AES), Japanese Encephalitis (JE) and heatwave conditions.During the meeting, civil surgeon Dr Jai Prakash Singh informed the DM that adequate medical arrangements have been put in place at all levels of healthcare facilities. A 10-bed AES PICU ward has been made operational at the sadar hospital, while five-bed AES wards equipped with mosquito nets have been set up at sub-divisional hospitals. At the block level, two-bed AES wards with mosquito nets have also been established to ensure prompt treatment.Officials said several rounds of training have been conducted for health personnel to deal with AES cases. At the state level, training was provided to hospital managers, district epidemiologists, programme coordinators, medical officers and paramedical staff. At the district level, master trainers have trained health workers and frontline staff.According to the health department, 39 medical officers, 49 paramedical staff, 16 block community managers, over 750 ASHA workers and facilitators, and several ambulance personnel have been trained in AES response and patient management. Additional training programmes are currently being conducted at the block level.

full_text:
GHHI launches South Asia hub to address India's extreme heat
India is dealing with a serious heat problem, with 2026 already seeing warm conditions in February.
Thanks to climate change, 57% of Indian districts are now considered high-risk for extreme heat.
To tackle this, a new South Asia Hub from the Global Heat Health Information Network (GHHIN) has launched, bringing together groups like CEEW and UNESCAP to find solutions.
Hub plans climate data early warnings
The hub wants to boost teamwork between governments, scientists, and local experts so India can handle rising temperatures better.
Dr. Arunabha Ghosh from CEEW points out that heat affects everything, from health to daily life, with over 44,000 heat stroke cases reported just in 2024.
The plan? Use climate data for smarter health responses and set up better early warning systems to protect people before things get dangerous.

title: Delhi-NCR to heat up after brief rain relief; temperature may reach 38°C by April 14 - Moneycontrol.com
full_text:
After a brief spell of rain and thunderstorms brought relief to residents, Delhi-NCR is now set to witness a steady rise in temperatures, with the mercury likely to touch 38°C by April 14, as per TOI.
The recent dip in temperature across Delhi, Noida, Ghaziabad, and nearby areas was caused by an active western disturbance that led to cloudy skies, rainfall, and gusty winds. Due to this, the national capital even recorded its coolest April day in nearly a decade, with the maximum temperature dropping significantly below normal levels.
However, this relief is expected to be short-lived. According to meteorological experts quoted by TOI, the western disturbance is weakening and moving away, leading to clearer skies and a shift towards dry weather conditions. As a result, temperatures are expected to rise gradually over the coming days.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has predicted that the maximum temperature will increase by around 6–8 degrees Celsius over the next week. The mercury is likely to hover between 30–32°C initially, before climbing to 35–37°C and eventually touching 36–38°C by April 14.
Weather officials have also indicated that once the influence of the western disturbance fades completely, dry westerly winds will return, further contributing to the rise in temperature. This pattern is typical for Delhi during April, when summer conditions begin to intensify.
Earlier this week, the IMD had issued alerts for thunderstorms and light rain in the region, which helped bring down both temperature and air pollution levels temporarily. With the skies now expected to clear, both heat and air quality levels may worsen again.
April usually marks the beginning of peak summer in Delhi, with temperatures often crossing 36°C and continuing to rise towards May. Experts warn that if dry conditions persist, heatwave-like situations could develop in parts of northwest India in the coming days.
Residents are advised to stay hydrated, avoid stepping out during peak afternoon hours, and take precautions against rising heat as Delhi-NCR transitions from rain-cooled weather to typical summer conditions.
